Me: Hello? Hello? Hey, how's it going? Hi. Can you hear me? I think you may be on mute. Hey, how are you, Jeff?  
Them: Yeah, sorry about that. Good. How are you? Sorry I'm late. I'm, like, trying to find a power cord and everything else here.  
Me: No, all good. All good. You just get into the office or co working space. Or just moving around.  
Them: Yeah, we just have. We have a lot of events going on on our floor, so just moving around. It's loud up there right now. I'm on a different floor in our building.  
Me: It's the modern office. Nobody gets that one space we all must share.  
Them: Figure out if I can plug in it is. That is very true.  
Me: Actually, I have two coworkers on my desk right now. To furry coworkers. So it's a pretty hostile work environment here as well.  
Them: There's definitely. What are they?  
Me: Two cats. Two cats. Two cats. Gary and avocado.  
Them: Nice, okay?  
Me: Do you have any pets of your own?  
Them: I do not. Yeah, not right now. My son has had different, smaller pets, but they've all not made it very par. I can't.  
Me: That's tough. Yeah, small pets.  
Them: Hamsters and stuff like that that are, you know, they don't live that long.  
Me: Yeah.  
Them: So. Currently no pets. I'm sure that'll change soon.  
Me: They get into their head soon enough. I guess the first time one of their friends. One of your friends gets a pet, it's game over for everyone else, right?  
Them: I think so, for sure.  
Me: To meet. I really appreciate your time. It was very cool to learn about Tech Buffalo and really appreciated the chance to just chat and get to know you.  
Them: Yeah, I appreciate it, too. I mean, We're charged with trying to figure out.  
Me: Hey. Sorry. You. Seem to have cut out. Hey. Sorry.  
Them: That was me.  
Me: Yep. You're back.  
Them: Yeah. What's going. On.  
Me: Maybe. Is this helping? Turning. So well.  
Them: All right, I switched wi fi.  
Me: Oh, nice. Okay.  
Them: I should be good now.  
Me: You're good. You're good. Yeah. I was like, we're doing so well. And then the wifi gods got in the way.  
Them: Yeah. So, anyway, like, we worked to develop, you know, Buffalo's kind of talent profile so that we have the resources that are necessary to grow. Everything from our enterprise companies ensuring that they, you know, not only want to stay here, but expand here to, you know, having the density of talent necessary for startups to find the right people that they need to grow. And, you know, within that kind of context, we're at this point where we either need to decide that we're going to build a product or we're going to buy a product. But we need, you know, we need to. I don't know how. Probably a poor term of putting it. Not that it's not professional now, but, like, just, like, professionalize our offerings. Like, add a, you know, easier way for everybody to get in and manage this. So, yeah, I was happy to connect and then learn more about where you're at and what's going on?  
Me: Totally. Totally. Absolutely. I mean, sometimes, or at least when I started down this path of starting this company and we decided, hey, we're going to focus on jobs and hiring, I remember being a little disappointed. And I was like, oh, it's just hiring. It's just talent. And over the last three years, I mean, honest to God believe this is one of the biggest challenges we're going to face in the next decade is how do we put square pegs and square holes, especially with the entire landscape shift being shifting sands under our feet. It's going to be really big. And I'm actually a little bit of a doomer, unfortunately, in that I don't believe that the ATS systems are really doing anything as far as scalably sorting talent. Right? They're basically just lying by omitting and not admitting that their tech sucks and it can't really work.  
Them: Yeah.  
Me: So that's sort of the position we found ourselves and in our belief was the way out of this is to a accept the resume is an inadequate data point, that any level of scalable decision making based on the resume is inherently going to be bad or limited.  
Them: Agreed.  
Me: And that's before you even consider that the marginal cost of bullshit is now zero, right? AI can just spit out the perfect resume for any job. And as long as someone's willing to look turn the or look the other way when submitting their application, all of a sudden it's on your head or your founder's heads to review those applications. That's time wasted. So our thinking was, on one hand, can we build a better way of getting the kind of data that helps us make better decisions? And that's what we turned our formerly career coaching product into. So it uses what is essentially Socratic dialogue, right, as a way of helping people exposit about themselves, who they are, what they've done. It collects that information, it breaks that down into sort of the little data labels that you need to have this mental model of each candidate, and then you can scan against that. So you could almost build out a community of, let's say, just the folks in Tech Buffalo, if they all have Careerspan accounts, you could scan against everyone in the community in one go. If a founder wanted to immediately hire and just know, hey, what's the level of talent? Or what's the current? Get a quick pulse check. Alternatively, they could put out jobs and it would be true. One click apply and I'm happy to show you the product as well, but it's very much meant to be. Let me read this resume and have to piece together what is actionable and whether to pay attention, whether to care about this person to a completely different like, oh, the information I'm getting is already actionable. I just need to decide whether I buy it or not. And that's what determines whether to bring the candidate into my pipeline or not.  
Them: Yeah. I mean, I think there's. A LinkedIn article I was reading, actually just yesterday about this very topic. It's called by Ann Lauer. It's called the job market as hell. And it's in the Atlantic, but it goes into essentially, like, all of the. All the problems that you had where there's just. There's just so much. To your point, the net cost is zero. There's a massive amount of noise in the market, and the thing that makes the difference is the thing that the common ATS is not solving. Right, which is like, do people actually have experience doing, doing the work? And even if you're an undergrad student, do you have experiences that demonstrate. You know, either the behavioral attributes that I'm looking for or even, like, proactive development of some of the skills. And that's harder to parse out in a resume and much easier to parse out if that person can just tell us a little bit about what they've done.  
Me: Totally. Totally. Totally. I mean, it is. In a weird way, it is just as simple as that. And to the extent that I think one of. The limitations in how a lot of people in HR tech normally view the situation is they sort of entirely take the employer's side and they buy into this belief that the hiring market needs to be adversarial. It is just a very silly perspective. I think that the interview process is essentially a compromise between getting to know someone and evaluating them. And to the extent that we want to do both, but both of them are in tension. It's very hard to pull off a normal interview process. The cool thing about something like Careerspan is because the candidate gets this ample opportunity to download themselves into the platform and sort of give that three dimensional sense of who this person is that can also be really easily queried because we're using large language models all the way through. It's just really simple for the employer to scan against that and actually for it to know the difference between, hey, this person is qualified in setting a that's relevant. But is not qualified for this other thing. Like the example my head of AI always uses is if you have knife skills, you can be a butcher or you could be a surgeon. Right. And those are two completely different things. You need a system that has a nuance to identify which is which. And that's, I think, what we're able to do.  
Them: Yeah, that makes sense. Well, I would love to see, like, a little bit of, like, how, like, how does this actually work when you get, you know, kids in and, like, for us, like, our main talent market, like, we're. We'll. We'll. I think get more into an adult focused market over time. But I would say we are heavily. What I would say is like pre career talent. I would say that begins really at the earliest right now, like grade 11 of high school and moves through, through all stages of college. So like your undergrad, your master's, your doctoral candidates. But it's really this pre career market that we're trying to parse out. You know, how do you identify the best there because some of the signals that used to be more reliable just aren't anymore.  
Me: Totally.  
Them: So.  
Me: No, a lot of the traditional signals are unreliable. And let's face it, even the traditional signals were kind of bullshit, right? Like, just because you went to a certain school has really no bearing or at least a minimal correlation with how good you're going to be at the job, right? At least if you have a system that is good enough at weeding out the obviously unqualified candidates. And I do think, anyway, I think the way that we have things set up is meant to be very pro candidate from the get go. So you submit a resume to get in here. This is Donna. Donna is a profile that we made as sort of a generic SaaS PM profile to get to the stage. We'll have dropped in a. By the way, if you're Silicon Valley fan, this is like. I made this whole multi account thing based on Silicon Valley characters. So this is supposed to be Jared's. Jared's sister. Yeah. Donatella Done. Right. But the point was super sincere, super hardworking, not built to brag. Right. Sort of like Jared's character was the point. And so you'll see this person that's done okay. And had obviously done well at work.  
Them: Yeah. I like that.  
Me: But a lot of the times their resume doesn't show that. And more importantly, they don't know themselves well enough to represent themselves that way, right? So the core user motion is telling stories where you can talk, you can type. So here this is sort of just throwing in some dummy text, but you can again, talk, type. It accepts 50 different languages if you want to input in your native language and outputs as English. And right off the bat, you get a lot of these. Really detailed insights based exactly on the stuff that you've said. And so it's based exactly off of the language that used the numbers that used. It will coach you to express certain things. Or actually, let me actually show you this. I was doing a dummy account. I was trying to do one yesterday where I was intentionally being really limited and kind of brusque in my responses. And you'll see here the whole point of it is to be very encouraging to give you different ways to really think about what you've done so that it is sort of co ideating your experience with you, which is kind of the gap. That most folks, especially that pre career stage, that's what they need because they don't have that natural experience being the best self advocate. At the same time you can do these vibe checks. So as an individual with an account, you can at any point drop in a job description and it will do an analysis of how qualified you are for that job. So in this case, this is actually.  
Them: Yeah. Like. The the candidate can do that.  
Me: The Canada can do that. Yeah. And it will do a semantic analysis. So it will actually break the JD down into the component skills and responsibilities and then assess you along each individual one. So it's actually going to make.  
Them: I like that.  
Me: Now, is it ultimately as arbitrary as anything else? Absolutely right. But to the extent that we aren't just using binary keyword yes or no and moving to an actual semantic understanding of the underlying skill is at present, is it not already a huge leap forward? It'll actually tell you, hey, for this particular job. Whoops. These are the gaps, Right. This is stuff that's on your resume, but you haven't told a story. Right? Maybe tell a story. Why does Agave need this? And what is the gap that you have? Right. Again, all super actionable and you can immediately click this, tell a story, and one story could actually cover. Multiple, so it's not like you have to do an individual story for each. Most folks can do three or four stories. That's a total of 60, 80 minutes at most. And they have a pretty fleshed out account that they can use to apply endlessly to Careerspan jobs. And the other cool thing.  
Them: So go. Go back to that real quick, like.  
Me: Sure.  
Them: So. How does it like? How are you determining stories needed again?  
Me: How are we determining.  
Them: Like, how is this. Sorry, how is this being determined? It's like customer solutions engineer there. And then it says, stories needed. That's from. That's from you parsing out, like, the job description into the component skills and, like, attributes, and then.  
Me: Right. That's right. And then the AI knowing the AI knowing that a, the job requires customer relationship management, B, that the individual had it on the resume that they submitted when they onboarded, but also that they haven't done a story to back it up. So our thinking is everything that you say that you have, you should have a story. To back it up in some capacity.  
Them: Yeah.  
Me: And that's sort of the way in which we encourage folks to actually flesh out. It's just a framework for them to use to fill out their talking points in their entirety. And so they can actually do that. But what we also give them over and above that is.  
Them: Right.  
Me: This narrative. So we'll tell them. Hey. How should you position yourself? Your product leader who excels at turning into clear, measurable wins is how you pair cognitive science. So again, it's doing a lot of that stitching together of the story for you. It's able to do a lot of that and tell you, hey, these are the things that you should watch out for. These are your weaknesses that they will talk about. These are opportunities that you have that you should pay attention to. These are concerns that you should have as a candidate about your candidacy. All super actionable. Now the flip side for your employers, because obviously, the goal, I presume, is to create a really powerful ecosystem. I presume is to have employers be just as engaged as the employees as the candidates. What we would also be able to offer is the sort of employer portals where folks could add jobs pretty easily, submit these jobs to your community and let folks apply with a single click, literally one click. It will do an initial evaluation. They'll get the opportunity to fill in more stories if they want to close the loop on anything. And then they click again, submit, and they end up over here. And so this is a dummy sort of dummy account. With a fake sort of job that we have up. And you'll actually see here both Donna and Donatella. Normally, Donatella, who has submitted no stories couldn't apply because we set a two story limit just for illustration purposes. You can actually see a pretty big difference. And this is the same resume, same candidate. It's just one has stories and one doesn't. Right. And so even visually, you can sort of indicate to employers, hey, this doesn't just tell you that this person is giving you a more complete picture of who you are. This is not a reflection of how qualified someone is. This is how much visibility do you have into them. Right. And that's the sort of big game changer. And so it's just a more neutral, it's less value judgment laden thing. And it is also more true to reality is that, hey, you're making this assessment based on how much relatively you know about Donna and just how confidently you're making that decision based on what you're seeing in front of you.  
Them: Gotcha. I like this. Tell me about. How do you check in on, like, it's reliability here of like the, you know, so the candidate goes in, they do this work. And the employer. Well, I guess I have a couple questions that are coming to mind. So the employer posts the job description. The candidate, you know, sees it. They see how they align. They can adjust it if they, if they see fit, and then they can apply. The employer then gets that. You know? The employer then gets that, you know, profile of candidates back. Can the employer like rank? Or does the employer have any variables that they can play with? Like, can they. Can they say, I want. You know? Tangible experience has demonstrated by a GitHub repository or something like that. Do they have?  
Me: We haven't. We haven't added those filters yet. We're open to them. This is very much a sort of V1. The way we've sort of been thinking about it is actually getting the coaching side product embedded in communities, because that has intrinsic value and a lot of upside. We actually sort of. Give and sort of on that basis, can actually also gauge, hey, how desirous is the community of trying to get hired through this platform. And at the same time, I think it's also just a really powerful way of illustrating how qualified the students are. Right. Because all of a sudden, you're not just going to be able to show them resumes, you're going to be able to send them actual, hey, I think this person's qualified. For this reason, and from there we could even transition you to actually scanning against running jobs against your community. In particular, I don't know if any of your monetization relies on recruiting fees from companies, but that's something that we've spoken to communities about that some have expressed an interest in.  
Them: Yeah, we're in la. We're in a really kind of interesting. I don't want to start from a pre market. Relative to how you would think about like a for profit business. We're kind of like in this three market phase where we're fully funded by foundations to do our work. So we're not. We're not. Our decisions aren't driven by the need to have profit. Like, outcomes. At this point, we're really just trying to build towards, like, a community. So. The blessing is we have some time to experiment here without having to worry about monetizing. Our kind of talent product, I guess. So, you know, really, our, you know, core deliverable is just value in any format. Possible. I like the. I mean, I see that. I see a tremendous amount of value. In the coaching that the candidate receives. And what I would tend. My curiosity that I have is would the candidates actually become better over time if the employ. Ers were able to curate. Within the job description, what they're actually looking for. Like, as they, as they start to parse candidates, can they start to figure out what are the most reliable signals and then disproportionately begin to weight those, which then provides signals back to the candidate marketplace that says employers value this, not that.  
Me: Right, Right. I mean, I love that your head went to. Well, how do we get it back to the candidates? All of a sudden they are able to reposition themselves. Right. Because that's what it's about. It's about improving. The transmission of that signaling between the employer to the candidate and vice versa. I couldn't agree more. I think it's something that we want to be able to do to let folks tune stuff. Right now what we can do is even as simply as a voice message, a founder can send that to us and our head of AI can adjust the weights on her end. So we have it very much within the realm of. Capability now and then, very much within the vision, within the near term vision. We're at a little bit of an interesting place as well at this point. Candidly, we're a little bit. We're having a bunch of folks approach us for sort of acquisition or integration, sort of partnership talks as well. So I think for us. Yeah. I bring that up to say, first of all, I want to keep all possibilities open because from our perspective, whether it's being in house somewhere or partnering with a variety of communities, because we have Emory's alumni association, we have a bunch of really cool communities that want to distribute Careerspan sort of thinking about it the same way that there's intrinsic value in the coaching tool, the other area that would also be of interest to you because again, the cool thing about TechBuffalo is you're actually trying to cultivate community and growth in an area you want folks to stay within. Buffalo, right? Naturally. The good thing about Careerspan is we've designed it to be a long term engagement product, and we see generally month on month, anywhere between 3 to 5% of folks will naturally log back in to share their stories. And because of that, you actually get this longitudinal view of who someone is. So you could have some work in Buffalo for 10 years and have 10 years worth of stories, experiences, anecdotes on Careerspan that gives them a huge edge. That also lets you again down the road more confidently place that person and more confidently tell people, hey, you could take a gamble on someone that you never seen in the Midwest or you could hire someone from right here that we have a completely verifiable track record. This person is not a North Korean spy, which I've had, like, four founders tell me that they've had what they believed was a foreign agent in their interview process, but anyway.  
Them: Yeah, no, that's. Some of our enterprise companies have talked about that here, which is wild, you know.  
Me: It's wild. It's wild. And, you know, I think the world is turning a little bit crazy and it's interesting. I don't know if you also pay attention to some of the acquisition or the sort of moves that companies in our space are making on an acquisition. M and A side everyone's going after credentialing and identity verification because that's, in my opinion, that's the low hanging fruit. Right? It's very easy if you have enough resources, very easy to validate identity. It's much harder to validate the je ne sais quoi who's going to be a good hire. Right. But that's the billion dollar, trillion dollar question is how do we actually match talent correctly with minimal time and energy spent.  
Them: Yeah. Yeah. And so, I mean, the direction that you're going, I think the reason why I don't know what your traction is in the marketplace. The reason I tend to think, without knowing much about you, that it probably is fairly strong. It's like the research that we've done. When trying to figure out the right product. As really especially pre career talent. Product or pre career talent. Everything feels like it's a black box. And it's like you're taking that black box and you're completely blowing it up. And that's such an important gift to be able to give to that free career talent. So they feel like there's some semblance of control when they, when they, when they hit the submit button.  
Me: Y. Es. Yeah. We want people to feel agency right? We want them to feel like they are. In fact, before you submit on Careerspan, we let you input. Hey, what do you want the employer to include? Like, C. Don't see. Do you want to, like, leave a note for them?  
Them: This resonates with me a lot.  
Me: So, yeah, we're all about that. I think it's. For us, it's very important that. The optimal outcome if someone sees a job and they go, actually, this is not for me. And they don't waste their time and employ. Don't waste his or her time and. Or the person sees a 90 and they go, yes, this is for me. And they're one of the one to three resumes. At most, a founder sees a day. But those are really high quality, very high bar sort of ones where they know this person has taken the time and the energy to, you know, exposit about themselves in the necessary ways.  
Them: And when we were going through this, it looked like there was even. Insight given to the applicant on the strengths of other people in the candidate pool.  
Me: We have some stuff to work with, so we. We do a little bit of a relative analysis here. Let me just pull that up. At this point. Yeah. So we'll do a general comparison, but this is, to be fair, this is relative to. I think this is generalized. I don't think this is relative to a specific cohort. I think it does this sort of calculation or an assessment for each role on the fly and then puts you relative to that.  
Them: Okay? Okay? Well, look, this is very intriguing to me. I know that we're. I know that we're at time. Give me a breakdown of, like, you know.  
Me: Good.  
Them: Cost structure, things like. Like, is there, like, what are we looking at?  
Me: Yeah. Yeah. So, you know, we, we have primarily been hoping to fund by charging companies and charging companies, essentially, these, like, These magic links, right? Magic links being the ability to distribute a job to someone using Careerspan versus, like, another ATS. So we generally charge like 200 or 250 for keeping those links active. The upside is for communities, we give that away for free if they're willing to work with us to promote us. To employers, and that acts as a nice win win, and it sort of keeps everything aligned. So if that's of interest, you know, and especially if you do have a number of employers that are already sort of keen to hire from your community, this could be a really nice way for us to sort of, for everyone to get what they want. At the same time, I know that you said that finances are a little bit less of a concern, and so if you are, if there's a different dimension that is more aligned with, like, just what works for you, but also is, like, optically better, I'm happy to work with something you know, like a two, three. I mean, when we've discussed it with universities, we've discussed a three to five dollar annual license. So if that gives you like context for what a potential like per person license would be, that would be the context there. But again, our preference would be to give it to the community for free and recharge employers to access or tap into the community.  
Them: So. I want to. Do you have anything that you have, like, any, like, light? I don't know. There's probably something on the website. I didn't do any homework ahead of this. I apologize. I normally do.  
Me: No, no, no. Good. I have. I have. So we. So it's, it's interesting with a lot of the communities we've spoken to, I have some community paraphernalia that are not pamphlets that I can send you so I can pop that over. But really, for communities, it's been pretty one. On one. Like with Emory's alumni association, for instance, we're rolling out all of their, like, wise hearts. It's a couple thousand folks as, like, a pilot for a couple of months, and then they're looking to. They're, like, essentially saying, okay, we'll look at a contract after that, so. It's been pretty flex so far. We've put most of our eggs in the trying to sell to employers basket, but our big, big priority right now is distribution, which is why we're still very interested in working with communities and getting our product into people's hands.  
Them: Yeah. Because, you know, I think about. I guess what I. What I'd be. I'd be interested in looking at what you have and then taking a little bit of time myself here to just, like, come back to you and come back to you with, like, the proper ask. Because I see two communities that we have.  
Me: Sure.  
Them: Maybe it's both. Maybe it's one over the other, depending upon, you know, listening to your value. Like the value that you seek out of this as well. And I think we have. We have an alumni community that comes out of our internship program. It's about five to 600 people.  
Me: Yeah.  
Them: That can be really interesting to help those folks see where the opportunities are in the community, how they align to them and look at the product in that space. And then of course, we have like the pre career talent, which is everybody that, you know, I talked about initially. So. But we have those two different areas and the more seasoned, Alan. And by more season, maybe they have a couple years of experience. Our goal is to ensure that they're not racing out of Buffalo. Once they have that, you know, two to three years. Two to three years of experience where now they can get recruited into a New York City or Chicago or, you know, you name it.  
Me: Right. That's the brain drain you're trying to avoid, right?  
Them: If they could see the opportunities here. Correct. So let me. Let me do some thinking about this. If you have anything that you can send me about, like community examples or things like that.  
Me: Yeah.  
Them: That would be helpful. And then when I get that, I'll reach back out to you, like, probably just like, with my calendly and say, like, let's just find something next week.  
Me: Yeah.  
Them: And that'll allow us figure out what some next steps are.  
Me: Amazing. Amazing. I'll send you that. Me: Amazing. Yeah. Absolutely it was. Pleasure is all mine.  
Them: I'll find him upstairs and thank him.  
Me: Yeah, absolutely.  
Them: All right. Well, hey, really appreciate it.

Me: Bye.
